title: L04 · Articles, Gender, Plural
objective: Learn the article system (el/la/los/las, un/una/unos/unas), the gender patterns that get you to 90% accuracy, and the gender TRAPS that catch French speakers (where Spanish and French disagree).
strategy: Spanish gender is easier than French because most words follow predictable -o/-a endings. The pain comes from (1) the -ma/-ema words that LOOK feminine but are masculine, and (2) words where Spanish and French disagree. Section 4 is the French-speaker survival guide.

sections.title: The article system
sections.note: Four forms each: singular/plural × masculine/feminine. Compare to French — Spanish has -s plurals (like English), and definite/indefinite parallel French exactly.

blocks.text: Definite article (the): el / la / los / las
blocks.audioText: el libro, la casa, los libros, las casas
blocks.rule: Compare French: le/la/les. Spanish adds masculine plural 'los' that French doesn't distinguish.

examples.translation: the book / the books — masc. / le livre / les livres
the house / the houses — fem. / la maison / les maisons
the water / the waters — TRAP: 'agua' is FEMININE but takes 'el' to avoid two a-sounds. Plural is 'las aguas'.

blocks.text: Indefinite article (a/an, some): un / una / unos / unas
blocks.audioText: un libro, una casa, unos libros, unas casas
blocks.rule: Spanish has plural indefinites (unos/unas = 'some') that French DOES NOT have (French would use 'des'). This is a Spanish-only feature.

examples.translation: a book / some books / un livre / des livres
a house / some houses / une maison / des maisons
some friends / des amis — French speakers: 'unos' = 'des' in plural

blocks.text: Mandatory contractions: a + el → al, de + el → del
blocks.audioText: Voy al cine. Vengo del trabajo.
blocks.rule: Like French (à + le → au, de + le → du), Spanish contracts these two combos. NEVER write 'a el' or 'de el' — always 'al' and 'del'. Exception: when 'El' is part of a proper name ('Voy a El Salvador').

examples.translation: I'm going to the cinema. / Je vais au cinéma.
I'm coming from work. / Je viens du travail.

sections.title: Gender — the 90% patterns
sections.note: Follow these endings and you'll guess right most of the time.

blocks.audioText: el libro, el carro, la casa, la mesa
blocks.rule: About 80% of Spanish nouns follow this. -o ending = masculine. -a ending = feminine.

examples.text: el libro, el museo, el barrio, el coche
examples.audioText: el libro, el museo, el barrio, el coche
examples.translation: book, museum, neighbourhood, car (oddly el coche — see below)

examples.text: la casa, la mesa, la silla, la cama
examples.audioText: la casa, la mesa, la silla, la cama

blocks.audioText: la nación, la decisión, la ciudad, la libertad, la actitud
blocks.rule: Words with these endings are ALWAYS feminine. Easy because they're cognates with French (-tion, -sion, -té, -tude).

blocks.audioText: el doctor, el viaje, el corazón, el problema, el planeta
blocks.rule: Watch out for -ma and -ta words from Greek — they LOOK feminine but are MASCULINE. These trip up everyone.

examples.translation: problem / le problème — TRAP: ends in -ma but masculine
planet / la planète — TRAP: -ta but masculine
day / le jour — TRAP: ends in -a but masculine!

sections.title: Plural patterns
sections.note: Plurals are mostly +s or +es. Much simpler than French.

blocks.audioText: casa → casas, libro → libros, café → cafés
blocks.rule: Just add -s like English (and unlike French, where the final -s is silent — in Spanish it's PRONOUNCED).

blocks.audioText: papel → papeles, ciudad → ciudades, mes → meses
luz → luces, pez → peces, vez → veces
blocks.rule: The -z changes to -c before -es. Same spelling rule as English changing 'wife' to 'wives'.

sections.title: Gender TRAPS for French speakers — where Spanish and French disagree
sections.note: These are real word-by-word mismatches. If you transfer the French gender, you'll be wrong. Memorise these high-frequency ones.

blocks.rule: Milk is feminine in Spanish, masculine in French. Same idea, different gender.

examples.translation: I want the milk cold. / Je veux le lait froid.

blocks.rule: Salt is feminine in Spanish, masculine in French.

examples.translation: Pass me the salt, please. / Passe-moi le sel, s'il te plaît.

blocks.rule: Blood is feminine in Spanish, masculine in French.

examples.translation: Blood is red. / Le sang est rouge.

blocks.rule: The sea is masculine in Spanish, feminine in French. Note: in poetic or specific contexts you may see 'la mar' but the default is 'el mar'.

examples.translation: The Mediterranean Sea is blue. / La Méditerranée est bleue.

blocks.text: el color (masc) ↔ la couleur (fem); la flor (fem) ↔ la fleur (fem — same)
blocks.rule: Color is masculine in Spanish, feminine in French. Flower is feminine in both. No safe shortcut — verify each new noun.

examples.translation: the red colour / la couleur rouge
the yellow flower / la fleur jaune

blocks.rule: Same gender — but note the written accent on 'á' (irregular stress).

blocks.text: el coche (masc, Spain) / el carro (LatAm) ↔ la voiture (fem)
blocks.rule: Car is masculine in Spanish, feminine in French. Spain says 'el coche', most of Latin America says 'el carro'. Both are masculine.

blocks.text: When in doubt, LOOK IT UP — don't transfer French gender
blocks.audioText: el día, el mapa, la mano, el problema, la radio
blocks.rule: Five high-frequency words to memorise: el día (day, masc despite -a), el mapa (map, masc despite -a), la mano (hand, fem despite -o!), el problema (masc despite -ma), la radio (fem despite -o).

examples.translation: day, hand — both break the -o/-a rule

examples.text: la radio / la foto / la moto
examples.audioText: la radio, la foto, la moto
examples.translation: radio, photo, motorcycle — feminine despite ending in -o (shortened from radiografía, fotografía, motocicleta)
